body_text:
Section 5.2 Definite Integrals of Trigonometric Functions

ategrals of Trigonometric Functions
Some of the nicest applications of the residue theorem concern the evaluati of definite integrals of real-valued functions. In this section we consider so straightforward examples that illustrate the underlying method. In la sections, we will develop this method using delicate analytical techniqu and evaluate more complicated integrals.

The examples in this section are all of the form
$$
\int_{0}^{2 \pi} F(\cos \theta, \sin \theta) d \theta
$$
where $F(\cos \theta, \sin \theta)$ is a rational function of $\cos \theta$ and $\sin \theta$ with real co ficients and whose denominator does not vanish on the interval $[0,2 \pi]$. I example, the integrals
$$
\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{d \theta}{2+\cos \theta} \text { and } \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{\cos ^{2}(2 \theta)}{4+2 \sin \theta \cos \theta} d \theta
$$
are of the form (1). Our goal is to transform the definite integral (1) into contour integral that can be evaluated using the residue theorem. For th purpose, let us recall Euler's identity:
$$
e^{i \theta}=\cos \theta+i \sin \theta, \quad \theta \text { any real number. }
$$

Using $-\theta$ in place of $\theta$, we get
$$
e^{-i \theta}=\cos \theta-i \sin \theta, \quad \theta \text { any real number } .
$$

Adding and subtracting the two identities, we obtain the familiar identit:
$$
\cos \theta=\frac{1}{2}\left(e^{i \theta}+e^{-i \theta}\right) \text { and } \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2 i}\left(e^{i \theta}-e^{-i \theta}\right) .
$$

As $\theta$ varies in the interval $[0,2 \pi]$, the complex number $z=e^{i \theta}$ traces $C_{1}($ the unit circle in the positive direction. This suggests transforming $t$ integral (1) into a contour integral, where the variable $z=e^{i \theta}$ traces $C_{1}($ Observing that $\frac{1}{z}=\frac{1}{e^{i \theta}}=e^{-i \theta}$, we obtain from (2) the key substitutions
$$
\cos \theta=\frac{1}{2}\left(z+\frac{1}{z}\right), \quad \text { and } \quad \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2 i}\left(z-\frac{1}{z}\right) .
$$

This is represented graphically in Figure 1. Also, from $z=e^{i \theta}$, we ha $d z=i e^{i \theta} d \theta=i z d \theta$ or

$\cos \theta$
$$
-i \frac{d z}{z}=d \theta
$$
